Guidelines.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals, according to two large prospective cohort studies of nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Research on moderate egg consumption in two large prospective cohort studies (nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women) found that up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports the study findings that moderate egg intake did not raise heart disease risk in the described populations.","population":"nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women","intervention":"up to one egg per day","comparator":"","outcome":"heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"For most people, cholesterol in food has a smaller effect on blood total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol than the mix of fats in the diet.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"a solid body of research shows that for most people, cholesterol in food has a smaller effect on blood levels of total cholesterol and harmful LDL cholesterol than does the mix of fats in the diet","entailment":"The quote conveys research evidence that dietary cholesterol influences blood lipids less than dietary fat composition.","population":"most people","intervention":"cholesterol in food","comparator":"mix of fats in the diet","outcome":"blood total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"A 2008 report from the Physicians’ Health Study supports that eating an egg a day is generally safe for the heart, but consuming much more may increase risk for heart failure later in life.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"While a 2008 report from the Physicians’ Health Study supports the idea that eating an egg a day is generally safe for the heart, it also suggests that going much beyond that could increase the risk for heart failure later in day","comparator":"eating much more than one egg per day","outcome":"risk for heart failure later in life","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"People who have protein sources)","comparator":"red meat or eggs","outcome":"cardiovascular and overall mortality","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Eggs are not the optimal breakfast choice but are not the worst; moderate to low intake is best for most people, emphasizing plant‑based protein options when possible.","claimType":"value","quote":"While eggs may not be the optimal breakfast choice, they are certainly not the worst, falling somewhere in the middle on the spectrum of food choice and heart disease risk. For those looking to eat a healthy diet, keeping intake of eggs moderate to low will be best for most, emphasizing plant‑based protein options when possible","entailment":"The quoted passage provides a normative recommendation to consume eggs in moderation and prioritize plant‑based proteins for a healthier diet.","population":"most people","intervention":"moderate to low egg intake","comparator":"","outcome":"heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"normative"}]}
